one_line_takeaway: Under a unified consensus evaluation (multiple static analyzers + LLM judge + executable unit tests) applied to Sven, SafeCoder, and PromSec, reported 'secure' rates of 65-99% collapse to true secure-and-functional rates of just 3-17% even without attack, and fall as low as 3.4% under simple black-box adversarial prompt perturbations -- because static analyzers systematically mislabel non-functional code (37-76% of outputs under attack) as secure.
key_finding: Static analyzers massively overestimate true security when functionality is required. In the unified (unattacked) setting: Sven's CodeQL-reported security of 66.3% collapses to 5.8% true secure-and-functional (11.4x overestimation), with 46.0% of outputs non-functional; SafeCoder's CodeQL 64.8% collapses to 3.0% (21.6x overestimation, 37.0% non-functional); PromSec's CodeQL 98.5% collapses to 13.3% (7.4x overestimation, 60.0% non-functional -- the worst of the three). Under the consensus metric (all analyzers + unit tests agree), baseline secure-and-functional rates were Sven 7.0%, SafeCoder 10.2%, PromSec 15.5%. Under adversarial attack within the unified framework (RQ3), rates collapsed further: under InverseComment, consensus secure-and-functional was Sven 9.3%, SafeCoder 9.4%, PromSec 17.6% (but PromSec's figure is inflated by non-functional code, since 70.8% of its outputs were non-functional); under StudentStyle, rates fell to Sven 3.4%, SafeCoder 4.3%, PromSec 15.2% (PromSec again with 76.0% non-functional outputs), and SafeCoder's generation rate itself dropped to 45.7% of tasks (frequent refusals). A distinct, counterintuitive finding for PromSec: under Phase-1 (its own Bandit-only metric), every adversarial perturbation tested INCREASED its reported security rate (baseline 88% -> up to 98% under DeadFunc200), which the authors attribute to PromSec's iterative repair loop trying harder on noisier/harder inputs -- but this apparent robustness is an artifact of the repair converging to non-functional code that trivially satisfies Bandit's syntactic patterns. Sven also showed a 'bidirectional' effect: the same attacks that degraded the SVENsec (secure) prefix's security rate (e.g., -13% under InverseComment on CodeGen-2B) simultaneously increased the SVENvul (insecure) prefix's security rate (e.g., +11.1% under InverseComment), evidencing that the prefix mechanism is entangled with, not independent of, prompt content.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is the first systematic, three-phase adversarial audit of three state-of-the-art secure code generation methods -- Sven (prefix-tuning), SafeCoder (LoRA instruction tuning), and PromSec (black-box prompt optimization via graph-guided GAN repair). The authors: (1) subject each method's original evaluation protocol to a suite of black-box, inference-time prompt attacks (naturalness reframing/StudentStyle, cue inversion/InverseComment, minimal-context SparseComment/SparseQuestion, semantic neutralization/SafeComment, insecure-hint injection/VulComment, and three dead-code-injection variants); (2) build a unified evaluation framework on the CodeSecEval benchmark (180 Python tasks, 44 CWE types, split into SecEval-Base [67 instances] and SecEval-Plus [113 instances]) that jointly scores security via a consensus of CodeQL, Bandit, and a GPT-4o LLM-judge alongside executable unit tests; and (3) re-run the adversarial attacks inside this unified framework to measure true joint secure-and-functional rates. The paper is explicitly an audit/benchmarking study, not a new defense method -- it proposes no new attack-resistant training technique, only the audit methodology and seven best-practice principles for future secure-code-generation research.
threat_model: Black-box, query-only adversary against a deployed secure code generation service (API or IDE plugin). The adversary has no access to model weights, gradients, or architecture; it manipulates only natural-language context surrounding the code task (comments, docstrings, issue text) while leaving the underlying task/tests fixed. The adversary's goal is to bypass the model's security alignment and force generation of insecure code (functional or non-functional), motivated by supply-chain/reputational harms to the provider and indirect poisoning risk in shared multi-tenant systems like IDE plugins (Section 3.1-3.2). This is a stated, explicit threat model in the paper (unlike, per the authors, Sven's own paper, which the audit criticizes for omitting one).
limitations: Stated by the authors: the unified CodeSecEval audit is restricted to Python because ground-truth executable test suites of the needed quality exist only for that language; extending to Java/C/C++ was explicitly left out of scope. PromSec's stochastic GPT-3.5-Turbo backend was evaluated with a single generation per prompt (following the original paper's protocol), so no sampling-distribution/variance estimate is available for it, unlike the deterministic Sven/SafeCoder artifacts. Security-analyzer coverage is limited to CodeQL, Bandit, and GPT-4o-as-judge; the paper itself (Principle 4) notes that adding tools like Semgrep/SonarQube or dynamic fuzzing would further tighten the ground truth. Only two representative attacks (InverseComment, StudentStyle) were carried through the full Phase-3 unified-adversarial evaluation, rather than the entire attack suite. The audited methods (Sven, SafeCoder, PromSec) were deliberately chosen for public artifact availability and adoption; newer methods (HexaCoder, SecCoder, PEFT-based tuning) were excluded for lacking reproducible artifacts, so the findings characterize this specific method trio, not secure code generation in general.

datasets_used: CodeSecEval (180 Python tasks, 44 vulnerability types; SecEval-Base: 67 code-completion instances derived from SecurityEval/CWE sources; SecEval-Plus: 113 code-generation instances from the 2023 CWE Top 25)
HumanEval (Pass@k functional evaluation for Sven and SafeCoder)
MBPP, MMLU, TruthfulQA (SafeCoder's original functional/general-performance benchmarks)
Custom CodeQL-scenario dataset (Sven's original security benchmark)
Custom Python/Java vulnerability datasets used with Bandit/SpotBugs (PromSec's original security benchmark)
quantitative_results: Sven (CodeGen-2B, SVENsec prefix): baseline security 93.7%, drops to 80.6% under InverseComment (-13%), 84.8% under StudentStyle (-9%), 91.6% under SparseQuestion (-2%)
Sven (SVENvul prefix, CodeGen-2B): InverseComment increases security rate from 35.2% to 46.3% (+11.1%)
SafeCoder (CodeLlama-7B): baseline security 89.0%, drops to 80.6% under InverseComment (-8.4%), 86.3% under StudentStyle (-2.7%)
SafeCoder functional: HumanEval Pass@1 35.9 / Pass@10 54.7; MBPP Pass@1 35.1 / Pass@10 48.5; MMLU 28.6; TruthfulQA 28.2
PromSec Phase-1: baseline Bandit security 88%, rises to 98% under DeadFunc200 (+10), 95% under InverseComment (+7); code-graph similarity 0.89; fuzzing pass rate 100% (20/20)

Unified baseline (Table 5/6): Sven CodeQL 66.3% vs. true secure-functional 5.8% (11.4x overestimate, 46.0% non-functional); SafeCoder CodeQL 64.8% vs. 3.0% true (21.6x, 37.0% non-functional); PromSec CodeQL 98.5% vs. 13.3% true (7.4x, 60.0% non-functional)
Consensus secure-and-functional (Table 6, no attack): Sven 7.0%, SafeCoder 10.2%, PromSec 15.5%
Under InverseComment attack, consensus secure-and-functional (Table 8): Sven 9.3% (generation rate only 63.0%), SafeCoder 9.4% (generation rate 77.8%), PromSec 17.6% (70.8% non-functional)
Under StudentStyle attack, consensus secure-and-functional (Table 10): Sven 3.4% (generation rate 55.5%), SafeCoder 4.3% (generation rate only 45.7%), PromSec 15.2% (76.0% non-functional)
Cross-method static-analyzer overestimation ranges: CodeQL 7.4x-21.6x, Bandit 6.1x-18.2x, GPT-4o 1.2x-1.5x
baselines_compared: SafeCoder (He et al., LoRA instruction tuning, white-box)
PromSec (prompt optimization + graph-guided GAN repair, black-box)
